prompt:
We keep re-implementing 'filter this JSON dump and sum by region' in every internal tool, so I want a tiny in-memory relational engine we can share. TypeScript, node only, no npm. The tests import db.ts and query.ts; split the internals (predicates, aggregates, join logic...) however you like.

db.ts — export class Database:
- table(name, columns, rows): registers a table. columns is the declared list (duplicates rejected); every row must have exactly those keys — a missing or extra key is an Error naming the offending column. Rows must be COPIED in (later mutation of the caller's objects must not affect queries). Re-registering a name is an Error naming it.
- tables() -> sorted names; columns(name) -> declared order; rowCount(name). Unknown names are Errors naming the table.

query.ts — export class Query, new Query(db, tableName) (unknown table throws right there). All builder methods chain and run() executes:
- run() -> array of plain row objects, always fresh copies.
- select(...cols): projects, result keys in the order asked. where(field, op, value): ops eq ne lt lte gt gte in ('in' needs an array value, anything else is an Error mentioning 'in'); stacked wheres AND. orderBy(field, dir='asc'|'desc'): stable, later calls are tie-breakers, bad dir is an Error naming it. offset(n) skips, then limit(n) trims — both demand non-negative integers (Error naming limit/offset). Unknown fields/columns/operators anywhere -> Error naming them. Note select is applied LAST: where/orderBy on a column you didn't select must work.
- join(otherTable, leftField, rightField): INNER join — unmatched rows on either side drop out, multiple matches multiply. Merged rows keep all left columns; a right column whose name collides gets prefixed '<table>.<col>' (e.g. users.id) and is addressable in where/select/orderBy under that name. Validate the table and both fields.
- groupBy(...fields) + agg(alias, fn, field?): fn is count (no field) | sum | avg | min | max. Output rows carry the group fields plus one key per alias, groups in FIRST-SEEN order of the incoming rows. sum/avg demand numbers (Error naming the column when it hits a string); min/max also handle strings. Aliases may not collide with group fields or other aliases. having(field, op, value) filters the aggregated rows; orderBy/select then see only group fields and aliases. where always runs BEFORE grouping; joins feed into grouping too.

Feel of it:

```ts
const db = new Database();
db.table('sales', ['id','region','amount'], rows);
new Query(db, 'sales')
  .where('amount', 'gte', 50)
  .groupBy('region')
  .agg('total', 'sum', 'amount')
  .having('total', 'gt', 150)
  .orderBy('total', 'desc')
  .run();
```

Pipeline order is fixed regardless of call order: join -> where -> group/agg -> having -> orderBy -> offset -> limit -> select. Acceptance: `node --test test_db.ts test_query.ts test_agg.ts` — don't edit those.
